knowledge.insight: KVD: 'I've weighted crankbaits for a long time. Adding weight helps in three ways: extra depth, better castability (flat-sided cranks cast like potato chips without it), and allows slower retrieve speed. In winter you want to crawl a crankbait as slow as possible.'

KVD: 'The balance point on a crankbait is right by the throat or just forward of the front hook hanger — that's where you add weight. Storm SuspenDots for small amounts, clip-on lead weights on the front hook hanger for more. Open the eye of a lead drop-shot weight as alternative.'

KVD: 'Buoyancy is determined by water temperature and elevation above sea level. Tuning at home doesn't translate to the lake. Start with a fairly heavy weight, check in the water, clip off weight with side cutters until you hit that sweet spot between suspending and a very slow fall.'
KVD: 'I'm a big believer in flat-sided crankbaits in winter — the KVD 1.5 Flat and Lucky Shad for cold water. Throw Lucky Shad on spinning equipment with 8lb fluoro for extra distance and depth.'
